name: Apostolic Fathers
description: The earliest post-apostolic writers who had direct or near-direct contact with the apostles. Their works—letters, homilies, and manuals like the Didache—show how the first Christian communities read Scripture before formal doctrinal categories developed. Key figures include Clement of Rome, Ignatius of Antioch, and Polycarp of Smyrna.

images.caption: The apostolic era — Pentecost and the early church
images.credit: Julius Schnorr von Carolsfeld · Public domain

name: Church Fathers
description: The great theologians who shaped Christian orthodoxy through the ecumenical councils. This era produced the major schools of interpretation—Alexandrian (allegorical) and Antiochene (literal-historical)—and defined core doctrines of Trinity and Christology. Key figures include Origen, Augustine, Chrysostom, Jerome, and Athanasius.

images.caption: Augustine of Hippo — the patristic era

name: Medieval
description: The era of monasticism, scholasticism, and the fourfold sense of Scripture (literal, allegorical, moral, anagogical). Theologians systematized patristic insights using Aristotelian philosophy, producing massive commentaries and theological summaries. Key figures include Thomas Aquinas, Bernard of Clairvaux, Anselm of Canterbury, and Peter Lombard.

name: Reformation
description: The Reformers broke from medieval allegorizing to emphasize the literal-grammatical sense, sola scriptura, and the perspicuity of Scripture. This era produced landmark commentaries and confessional documents that still shape Protestant interpretation today. Key figures include Martin Luther, John Calvin, Huldrych Zwingli, and Thomas Cranmer.

images.caption: Martin Luther — the Reformation
images.credit: Lucas Cranach the Elder · Public domain

name: Modern Critical
description: The rise of historical-critical methods—source criticism, form criticism, and redaction criticism—transformed biblical studies into an academic discipline. Scholars investigated the human origins and literary history of biblical texts while others mounted robust theological responses. Key figures include Julius Wellhausen, Karl Barth, Gerhard von Rad, and Rudolf Bultmann.

images.caption: Modern era — the Dead Sea Scrolls discovery

name: Contemporary
description: Contemporary scholarship integrates historical-critical insights with literary, canonical, and theological approaches. New perspectives on Paul, Ancient Near Eastern backgrounds, and discourse analysis have reshaped longstanding debates. Key figures include N.T. Wright, D.A. Carson, G.K. Beale, and Tremper Longman III.

images.caption: Contemporary scholarship — digital access to ancient texts
